refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry available to downstream planning and execution functions. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design iteration.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite spatial model exists in a defined format (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature set) and can be queried by planning/execution functions to retrieve feature geometry and attributes within specified accuracy tolerances.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (e.g., structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and exclusion zones per operational policy.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (CR-NGHE-006) remains composite; the sub-FRs are atomic and ready for independent DP and IM assignment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured geometric model (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with semantic labels and metadata; expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign each zone a feature inventory (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a graph of zone nodes with edge constraints; persist in a queryable schema.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a rule-based symbolic representation (e.g., logic rules or ontology) that encodes spatial relationships, safety constraints, and operational policies; derive a queryable model on demand by evaluating rules against sensor observations.

DP4: Establish a real-time streaming pipeline that continuously ingests sensor data, applies feature extraction and classification, and maintains an incrementally updated spatial model; expose model state via subscription/polling interface for planning/execution.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to a hybrid approach: use pre-defined templates (e.g., site layout blueprints, CAD models) as a baseline; augment with live sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features; merge into a composite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid baseline-augmented model. Establish a composite spatial representation by anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, or prior maps) and augmenting with live multi-modal sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features. Fuse baseline and live data into a unified, queryable geometric and semantic model. Expose model state and feature queries via a standardized interface for planning and execution consumers. Maintain model freshness and consistency through incremental updates and conflict resolution policies aligned with safety and operational constraints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality selection and feature extraction logic—mitigated by abstracting sensor input as a generic observation stream and keeping extraction logic modular.

Dependency on baseline template availability and accuracy—mitigated by graceful degradation: if baseline unavailable, fall back to DP1 (pure sensor fusion); if baseline inaccurate, detection of deviations triggers re-calibration.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning/execution cycle time—mitigated by decoupling via asynchronous query interface; consumers poll or subscribe at their own cadence.
Potential coupling to specific safety/operational policy encoding—mitigated by externalizing policies as queryable constraints rather than hard-coding into model structure.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: baseline provision (external), live sensing (pluggable), fusion logic (centralized), and policy enforcement (declarative). The hybrid approach reduces brittleness compared to pure sensor fusion (DP1) or pure symbolic reasoning (DP3), and supports incremental model refinement without full re-acquisition.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite baseline (blueprint, CAD, or prior map) is available and reasonably accurate at mission start. RISK: If baseline is stale, missing, or significantly inaccurate, fusion may produce misleading composite model. MITIGATION: Implement baseline validation checks; flag high-confidence deviations; support manual override.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated. RISK: Sensor failures, miscalibration, or occlusions degrade feature detection. MITIGATION: Implement sensor health monitoring; use redundancy where safety-critical; graceful degradation to available modalities.

ASSUMPTION: Feature extraction and semantic labeling can be automated or semi-automated. RISK: Misclassification of hazards or features leads to unsafe planning. MITIGATION: Conservative labeling (over-report hazards); human-in-the-loop validation for high-risk features; traceability of labeling confidence.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and queryable. RISK: Policies are ambiguous, incomplete, or conflict with model representation. MITIGATION: Explicit policy schema; validation against model; escalation for unresolvable conflicts.

ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency and eventual consistency. RISK: Stale model leads to unsafe or inefficient decisions. MITIGATION: Define acceptable model age; implement update prioritization; alert consumers to model freshness.

ASSUMPTION: Composite model can be efficiently queried and updated in real-time. RISK: Model complexity or update frequency exceeds computational budget. MITIGATION: Hierarchical model representation; lazy evaluation; caching; spatial indexing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate three sub-concerns: (1) Baseline provisioning and validation (DP5a), (2) Live feature detection and anomaly flagging (DP5b), and (3) Fusion, conflict resolution, and query interface (DP5c). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries (IM layer) and allows independent evolution of baseline sources, sensor pipelines, and model consumers. Additionally, introduce a formal model schema (e.g., GeoJSON + semantic tags + policy constraints) to ensure interoperability and auditability. Define acceptance criteria for model freshness, feature detection recall/precision, and policy compliance.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation engine that ingests baseline worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) and fuses them with live multi-modal sensor observations. Maintains a unified geometric and semantic model of the worksite, detects deviations and dynamic hazards, resolves conflicts between baseline and live data, and exposes queryable model state and feature data via standardized interfaces to planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests live multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, thermal, etc.) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to augment baseline model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Exposes queryable model state, feature queries, and geometric/semantic data to planning and execution consumers (e.g., path planners, task schedulers).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Publishes detected deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features to Safety Services for constraint enforcement and operational decision-making.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) and synchronizes model state across distributed nodes if multi-agent coordination is required.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for spatial representation, sensor fusion, baseline-augmented modeling, and model query exposure. All core DP logic (baseline anchoring, live augmentation, conflict resolution, model freshness) resides here. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (Vehicle/Teleoperation) and safety alert publication (Safety Services) cross boundaries via well-defined interfaces.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform/sensor provider, not a model fusion engine. Placing DP here would scatter baseline template management, conflict resolution, and model query logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling and poor reusability across multiple vehicles or teleoperation modes.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a spatial reasoning engine. Embedding baseline-augmented modeling here would conflate operator control logic with model fusion, reducing modularity and making the model inaccessible to autonomous planning systems.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a constraint enforcement and alerting layer, not a model fusion engine. While Safety Services consumes model outputs (hazard detection), it should not own baseline template management, sensor fusion, or model state maintenance. Placing DP here inverts the dependency and couples safety logic to model internals.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the single, most cohesive boundary for DP5. It is purpose-built to integrate baseline spatial data with live sensor observations, maintain a unified queryable model, and expose that model to downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety). The DP's core concerns—baseline anchoring, multi-modal fusion, conflict resolution, model freshness, and query exposure—are all native to NGHE's domain. External dependencies (telemetry ingestion, safety alert publication, baseline template receipt) are minimal and cleanly separated via standardized interfaces.
containment_risks: Risk: If baseline templates (blueprints, CAD) are managed by an external system (e.g., a project management or asset database), NGHE Solution must establish a clear inbound interface (Network Link or new dedicated interface) to fetch and version templates. Failure to do so could lead to stale or inconsistent baseline data.

Risk: If multiple vehicles or teleoperation instances feed telemetry concurrently, NGHE Solution must implement robust conflict resolution and incremental update policies to prevent race conditions or model divergence. This requires careful synchronization logic and should be documented in the DP refinement.

Risk: If Safety Services or planning consumers expect real-time model updates with strict latency bounds, NGHE Solution must be architected to support incremental, low-latency model refreshes. Batch processing or delayed updates could violate operational constraints.

Risk: If the baseline template format or sensor modalities change frequently, NGHE Solution's abstraction layer (DP) must be flexible enough to accommodate new data sources without requiring module boundary changes. Poor abstraction could lead to tight coupling to specific sensor types or template formats.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable through acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) coverage of agreed site features. NGHE Solution's IM boundary explicitly owns model generation, fusion, and query exposure, making verification traceable to module outputs and interfaces.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single, unambiguous owner of the FR. The IM boundary encompasses baseline template ingestion, multi-modal sensor fusion, conflict resolution, model state maintenance, and query exposure—all necessary to satisfy the FR. External dependencies (telemetry input, safety alert output, baseline template receipt) are cleanly separated via named interfaces, reducing allocation ambiguity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (Hybrid baseline-augmented model) directly and completely addresses the FR. The DP specifies the solution approach: anchor to pre-defined worksite templates, augment with live multi-modal sensor observations, fuse into a unified queryable model, and expose via standardized interfaces. The DP's core mechanisms (baseline anchoring, live augmentation, deviation detection, conflict resolution, model freshness, query exposure) are logically sound and sufficient to satisfy the FR's requirement for machine-readable spatial representation. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Baseline template management: If blueprint/CAD/prior map ingestion is not fully owned by NGHE Solution (e.g., templates are fetched on-demand from an external asset database without version control or caching within NGHE), template staleness or inconsistency could propagate into the fused model. Recommend explicit ownership of template versioning and lifecycle within NGHE IM boundary.

Conflict resolution policy: The DP specifies 'conflict resolution policies aligned with safety and operational constraints,' but the IM boundary description does not detail who owns the policy definition (Safety Services? Operations? NGHE?). If policy ownership is diffused, NGHE may lack authority to enforce consistent conflict resolution, leading to model divergence. Recommend explicit policy ownership and update mechanism within NGHE or a clear delegation interface.

Real-time model freshness: The DP requires 'incremental updates and conflict resolution' to maintain freshness. If telemetry ingestion latency, fusion processing time, or query response time are not architected to meet operational latency constraints (e.g., planning cycle time), the model may become stale relative to dynamic hazards. Recommend explicit latency budgets and performance acceptance criteria for NGHE Solution.

Multi-modal sensor abstraction: The DP references 'live multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, thermal, etc.)' but does not specify how NGHE abstracts over sensor modality differences. If sensor-specific fusion logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE, or if new sensor types require NGHE module changes, diffusion risk increases. Recommend a clear sensor abstraction layer (e.g., normalized observation schema) owned by NGHE.

Safety alert publication: The DP specifies 'Expose model state and feature queries via a standardized interface' and 'Publish detected deviations, dynamic hazards.' If Safety Services defines hazard detection thresholds or alert criteria, and NGHE must implement them, ownership of hazard semantics could be diffused. Recommend explicit contract (interface specification) between NGHE and Safety Services for hazard definitions and alert triggers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits excellent containment (0.95 score) for DP5. The module boundary is purpose-built for baseline-augmented spatial modeling and is the natural home for all core DP logic: baseline anchoring, multi-modal fusion, conflict resolution, model state maintenance, and query exposure. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating low diffusion (0.05 score). However, containment quality depends critically on resolving the five diffusion risks above: (1) baseline template ownership and versioning, (2) conflict resolution policy authority, (3) real-time freshness latency budgets, (4) sensor abstraction layer design, and (5) hazard detection semantics contract. If these are not explicitly addressed in DP refinement and interface specifications, diffusion risk will increase post-implementation.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP5 to explicitly specify baseline template lifecycle management: Define who owns template versioning, caching, and synchronization (NGHE or external system). Establish a clear inbound interface (Network Link or dedicated 'BaselineTemplateService') with version control and conflict resolution semantics.

Define conflict resolution policy ownership and update mechanism: Specify whether conflict resolution policies (baseline vs. live data priority, hazard thresholds, feature precedence) are owned by NGHE, Safety Services, or Operations. If delegated, establish a clear policy update interface and versioning scheme.

Establish real-time model freshness acceptance criteria: Define latency budgets for telemetry ingestion, fusion processing, and query response relative to planning cycle time and dynamic hazard detection requirements. Document these as non-functional requirements for NGHE Solution.

Design and document sensor abstraction layer: Specify a normalized observation schema (e.g., point clouds, semantic labels, confidence scores) that NGHE uses internally to abstract over LiDAR, camera, thermal, and other modalities. This reduces coupling to specific sensor types and enables modular addition of new sensors.

Formalize hazard detection contract between NGHE and Safety Services: Define the interface for hazard definitions, detection thresholds, and alert triggers. Specify whether NGHE detects hazards (deviations, dynamic obstacles) and Safety Services enforces constraints, or whether Safety Services defines detection logic and NGHE implements it. Document this in a formal interface specification.

Add acceptance criterion for model coverage and feature agreement: Expand the acceptance criteria to specify which worksite features (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment) must be represented in the model and with what fidelity (geometric precision, semantic labels). This ensures NGHE Solution's output is verifiable against operational requirements.

Document incremental update and synchronization strategy: Specify how NGHE handles concurrent telemetry streams from multiple vehicles/teleoperation instances, including ordering guarantees, conflict resolution, and consistency models (eventual, strong, causal). This is critical for multi-agent scenarios.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass, indicating the FR is well-formed and the DP is logically sound. However, the DP-IM gate identifies five critical diffusion risks that, if unresolved, will propagate into implementation and compromise safety, consistency, and operational effectiveness. Given high safety criticality, these risks must be addressed before decomposing to sub-FRs. Revising the current level (DP and IM specifications) to resolve diffusion risks is more efficient than proceeding to sub-FRs and discovering ownership/interface gaps later. The recommended revisions are architectural (ownership, interfaces, latency budgets, abstraction layers) and must be locked in at this level to ensure sub-FR decomposition inherits a sound, unambiguous design context.

revise.why: Five diffusion risks require explicit resolution in DP refinement and IM boundary specification before sub-FR derivation: (1) Baseline template lifecycle management and ownership must be clarified—NGHE must explicitly own template versioning, caching, and synchronization, or a clear external service interface must be defined. (2) Conflict resolution policy authority must be assigned—specify whether NGHE owns policy definition or delegates to Safety Services/Operations, with a formal update interface. (3) Real-time model freshness acceptance criteria (latency budgets for telemetry ingestion, fusion, query response) must be documented as non-functional requirements tied to planning cycle time and hazard detection latency. (4) Sensor abstraction layer (normalized observation schema) must be designed and documented to reduce coupling to specific sensor modalities and enable modular sensor addition. (5) Hazard detection contract between NGHE and Safety Services must be formalized—specify detection logic ownership, threshold definitions, and alert trigger semantics. Additionally, acceptance criteria for model coverage and feature agreement should be expanded to specify which worksite features must be represented and with what fidelity. These revisions ensure DP5 and NGHE IM boundary are unambiguous, traceable, and safe before sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) are available and can be ingested by NGHE Solution; ownership and versioning responsibility is clarified in revised DP/IM.

Conflict resolution policies (baseline vs. live data priority, hazard thresholds, feature precedence) are either owned by NGHE or delegated via a formal interface; policy updates are versioned and traceable.

Telemetry ingestion, multi-modal fusion, and query response latencies can be architected to meet operational planning cycle time and dynamic hazard detection requirements; latency budgets are defined and measurable.

A normalized sensor abstraction layer (e.g., point cloud + semantic labels + confidence scores) can be designed to abstract over LiDAR, camera, thermal, and future sensor modalities without requiring NGHE module changes for each new sensor type.

Hazard detection semantics (what constitutes a deviation, dynamic obstacle, or safety-critical feature) are jointly defined by NGHE and Safety Services; ownership of detection logic vs. constraint enforcement is clear.

Multi-agent scenarios (concurrent telemetry from multiple vehicles/teleoperation instances) are within scope; NGHE model synchronization strategy (ordering, conflict resolution, consistency model) is architected and documented.
Worksite feature coverage and fidelity requirements are operationally defined (e.g., obstacles within 0.5 m, hazard zones with ±1 m precision, semantic labels for access points, equipment locations).
open_risks: Baseline template staleness or inconsistency: If template versioning and lifecycle are not owned by NGHE or a clear external service, templates may diverge from operational reality, corrupting the fused model. Mitigation: Assign explicit ownership and implement version control + caching within NGHE IM boundary.

Conflict resolution policy diffusion: If policy ownership is split between NGHE, Safety Services, and Operations, NGHE may lack authority to enforce consistent conflict resolution, leading to model divergence and safety violations. Mitigation: Formalize policy ownership and update mechanism; document in interface specification.

Real-time model freshness failure: If latency budgets are not architected or monitored, the model may become stale relative to dynamic hazards, delaying hazard detection and planning response. Mitigation: Define and validate latency budgets for telemetry ingestion, fusion, and query response; establish performance acceptance criteria.

Sensor modality coupling: If sensor-specific fusion logic is scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE, or if new sensor types require NGHE module changes, maintenance burden and diffusion risk increase. Mitigation: Design and enforce a normalized sensor abstraction layer; document sensor interface contract.

Hazard detection semantics ambiguity: If hazard definitions, thresholds, and alert criteria are not jointly specified by NGHE and Safety Services, NGHE may detect hazards that Safety Services does not recognize, or vice versa, leading to missed or false alerts. Mitigation: Formalize hazard detection contract; specify detection logic ownership and threshold versioning.

Multi-agent model consistency: If concurrent telemetry from multiple vehicles/teleoperation instances is not architected with clear ordering, conflict resolution, and consistency guarantees, the model may diverge across agents, leading to inconsistent planning and safety decisions. Mitigation: Document incremental update and synchronization strategy; specify consistency model (eventual, strong, causal).
evidence_needed: Revised DP5 specification with explicit baseline template lifecycle management: Define template versioning, caching, synchronization, and ownership (NGHE or external service). Document inbound interface (Network Link or dedicated BaselineTemplateService) with version control semantics.

Conflict resolution policy specification: Document policy ownership (NGHE, Safety Services, or Operations), policy definitions (baseline vs. live priority, hazard thresholds, feature precedence), and update mechanism (versioning, approval workflow). Formalize in interface specification.

Real-time model freshness acceptance criteria: Define latency budgets for telemetry ingestion, fusion processing, and query response relative to planning cycle time (e.g., 'model freshness ≤ 2 s for dynamic hazards, ≤ 10 s for static features'). Document as non-functional requirements for NGHE Solution.

Sensor abstraction layer design: Specify normalized observation schema (e.g., point clouds with semantic labels and confidence scores). Document how NGHE abstracts over LiDAR, camera, thermal, and future modalities. Provide sensor interface contract (input format, metadata, quality metrics).

Hazard detection contract (NGHE ↔ Safety Services): Formalize interface specification defining hazard definitions, detection thresholds, alert triggers, and ownership of detection logic vs. constraint enforcement. Document versioning and update mechanism for hazard definitions.

Expanded acceptance criteria for model coverage and feature agreement: Specify which worksite features (obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment) must be represented, with what geometric precision (e.g., ±0.5 m for obstacles), semantic labels, and confidence thresholds. Provide test cases for feature coverage verification.

Incremental update and synchronization strategy: Document how NGHE handles concurrent telemetry streams from multiple vehicles/teleoperation instances, including ordering guarantees, conflict resolution, and consistency model (eventual, strong, causal). Provide examples for multi-agent scenarios.

Interface specifications for all external dependencies: Formalize Telemetry (input schema, latency SLA), Tasking (query interface, response format), SafetyAlert (hazard definitions, alert format), Network Link (baseline template service, version control), and any new interfaces (BaselineTemplateService, PolicyUpdateService, SensorAbstractionService). Include version control and deprecation strategies.
